name: Janet Jackson
description:
'''Janet Damita Jo Jackson''' (born May 16, 1966) is an American singer, songwriter, actress, and dancer. Considered a [[pop icon]], she is known for her innovative, socially conscious and sexually provocative records, as well as elaborate stage shows. Jackson's sound and choreography became a catalyst in the growth of [[MTV]], enabling her to rise to prominence while breaking [[Gender inequality in the United States|gender]] and [[Racism against Black Americans|racial]] barriers in the process. Lyrical content that concerned social issues and deeply felt experiences contributed to the appeal of her work to the youth audience.

The tenth and youngest child of the [[Jackson family]], Jackson starred in multiple television shows throughout the 1970s and early 1980s, including ''[[Good Times]]'', ''[[Diff'rent Strokes]]'' and ''[[Fame (1982 TV series)|Fame]]''. After signing with [[A&M Records]] in 1982, Jackson achieved global stardom with the albums ''[[Control (Janet Jackson album)|Control]]'' (1986) and ''[[Janet Jackson's Rhythm Nation 1814|Rhythm Nation 1814]]'' (1989). These projects led to crossover success in [[popular music]] and influenced the development of the [[new jack swing]] fusion genre. In the 1990s, Jackson became one of the highest-paid musicians, signing two of the [[List of largest music deals|largest music deals of all time]] with [[Virgin Records]]. She cemented her status as a [[sex symbol]] with a leading role in the film ''[[Poetic Justice (film)|Poetic Justice]]'' (1993) and the albums ''[[Janet (album)|Janet]]'' (1993) and ''[[The Velvet Rope]]'' (1997).

Jackson achieved her [[List of Billboard Hot 100 chart achievements and milestones#Most number-one songs|tenth US number-one single]] with the [[All for You (Janet Jackson song)|title track]] from her seventh studio album, ''[[All for You (Janet Jackson album)|All for You]]'' (2001). Following [[Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy|the controversy]] surrounding her [[Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show|2004 Super Bowl halftime show]], Jackson experienced a career decline. She faced an industry [[blacklisting]] under the direction of [[Les Moonves]], then-CEO of [[CBS]], resulting in reduced radio airplay, televised promotion, and sales figures of her music catalog. The aftermath impacted the commercial performance of her eighth studio album, ''[[Damita Jo (album)|Damita Jo]]'' (2004). Over the next decades, Jackson continued to perform and release new music, including the studio albums ''[[20 Y.O.]]'' (2006), ''[[Discipline (Janet Jackson album)|Discipline]]'' (2008), and ''[[Unbreakable (Janet Jackson album)|Unbreakable]]'' (2015). Her tenth concert tour, [[Janet Jackson: Together Again|Together Again Tour]] (2023–2024), became the highest-grossing tour of her career.

Jackson is one of the [[List of best-selling music artists|best-selling music artists of all time]], with estimated sales of over 100 million records. She holds the record for the most consecutive top-ten entries on the US [[Billboard Hot 100|''Billboard'' Hot 100]] by a female artist (18) and remains the only artist in the history of the chart to have seven singles from one album—''Rhythm Nation 1814''—peak within the top five positions. ''[[Billboard (magazine)|Billboard]]'' ranked her among the highest-charting musicians in history and the second-most successful artist of the 1990s. [[List of awards and nominations received by Janet Jackson|Her accolades]] include five [[Grammy Awards]], 11 [[Billboard Music Awards|''Billboard'' Music Awards]], 11 [[American Music Awards]], 10 [[MTV Video Music Awards]] and eight ''[[Guinness World Records]]'' entries. In 2019, she was inducted into the [[Rock and Roll Hall of Fame]].
